question:
Problem A-2
Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on the constant \( \boldsymbol{k} \) for the existence of a continuous real valued function \( f(x) \) satisfying \( f(f(x))=k x^{9} \) for all real \( x \).
solution:
A-2.
The condition is \( k \geqslant 0 \). If \( k \geqslant 0 \), one sees that \( f(x)=\sqrt[4]{k x^{3}} \) satisfies \( f(f(x))=k x^{9} \). For the converse, we note that \( f(f(x))=k x^{9} \) for all real \( x \) with \( k \neq 0 \) implies that \( f \) takes on all real values since \( k x^{9} \) does and implies that \( f \) is one-to-one since \( f(a)=f(b) \) leads to \( k a^{9}=f(f(a))=k b^{9} \) and hence \( a=b \). But a continuous one-to-one function \( f \) from the real numbers \( \boldsymbol{R} \) onto itself must be strictly monotonic. Also, if \( f \) is monotonic, either always increasing or always decreasing, \( f(f(x)) \) will always be increasing and so cannot equal \( k x^{9} \) if \( k<0 \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-2
Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on the constant \( \boldsymbol{constantk} \) for the existence of a continuous real valued function \( functionf(variablex) \) satisfying \( functionf(functionf(variablex))=constantk variablex^{9} \) for all real \( variablex \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-2.
The condition is \( constantk \geqslant 0 \). If \( constantk \geqslant 0 \), one sees that \( functionf(variablex)=\sqrt[4]{constantk variablex^{3}} \) satisfies \( functionf(functionf(variablex))=constantk variablex^{9} \). For the converse, we note that \( functionf(functionf(variablex))=constantk variablex^{9} \) for all real \( variablex \) with \( constantk \neq 0 \) implies that \( functionf \) takes on all real values since \( constantk variablex^{9} \) does and implies that \( functionf \) is one-to-one since \( functionf(variablea)=functionf(variableb) \) leads to \( constantk variablea^{9}=functionf(functionf(variablea))=constantk variableb^{9} \) and hence \( variablea=variableb \). But a continuous one-to-one function \( functionf \) from the real numbers \( \boldsymbol{realsetr} \) onto itself must be strictly monotonic. Also, if \( functionf \) is monotonic, either always increasing or always decreasing, \( functionf(functionf(variablex)) \) will always be increasing and so cannot equal \( constantk variablex^{9} \) if \( constantk<0 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-2
Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on the constant \( \boldsymbol{quartzveil} \) for the existence of a continuous real valued function \( meadowlark(riverdelta) \) satisfying \( meadowlark(meadowlark(riverdelta))=quartzveil riverdelta^{9} \) for all real \( riverdelta \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-2.
The condition is \( quartzveil \geqslant 0 \). If \( quartzveil \geqslant 0 \), one sees that \( meadowlark(riverdelta)=\sqrt[4]{quartzveil riverdelta^{3}} \) satisfies \( meadowlark(meadowlark(riverdelta))=quartzveil riverdelta^{9} \). For the converse, we note that \( meadowlark(meadowlark(riverdelta))=quartzveil riverdelta^{9} \) for all real \( riverdelta \) with \( quartzveil \neq 0 \) implies that \( meadowlark \) takes on all real values since \( quartzveil riverdelta^{9} \) does and implies that \( meadowlark \) is one-to-one since \( meadowlark(cloudstone)=meadowlark(lanternfog) \) leads to \( quartzveil cloudstone^{9}=meadowlark(meadowlark(cloudstone))=quartzveil lanternfog^{9} \) and hence \( cloudstone=lanternfog \). But a continuous one-to-one function \( meadowlark \) from the real numbers \( \boldsymbol{amberfield} \) onto itself must be strictly monotonic. Also, if \( meadowlark \) is monotonic, either always increasing or always decreasing, \( meadowlark(meadowlark(riverdelta)) \) will always be increasing and so cannot equal \( quartzveil riverdelta^{9} \) if \( quartzveil<0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-2
Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on the constant \( \boldsymbol{changeable} \) for the existence of a continuous real valued function \( malfunction(constantvalue) \) satisfying \( malfunction(malfunction(constantvalue))=changeable\, constantvalue^{9} \) for all real \( constantvalue \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-2.
The condition is \( changeable \geqslant 0 \). If \( changeable \geqslant 0 \), one sees that \( malfunction(constantvalue)=\sqrt[4]{changeable\, constantvalue^{3}} \) satisfies \( malfunction(malfunction(constantvalue))=changeable\, constantvalue^{9} \). For the converse, we note that \( malfunction(malfunction(constantvalue))=changeable\, constantvalue^{9} \) for all real \( constantvalue \) with \( changeable \neq 0 \) implies that \( malfunction \) takes on all real values since \( changeable\, constantvalue^{9} \) does and implies that \( malfunction \) is one-to-one since \( malfunction(settlednum)=malfunction(stablequantity) \) leads to \( changeable\, settlednum^{9}=malfunction(malfunction(settlednum))=changeable\, stablequantity^{9} \) and hence \( settlednum=stablequantity \). But a continuous one-to-one function \( malfunction \) from the real numbers \( \boldsymbol{imaginaryset} \) onto itself must be strictly monotonic. Also, if \( malfunction \) is monotonic, either always increasing or always decreasing, \( malfunction(malfunction(constantvalue)) \) will always be increasing and so cannot equal \( changeable\, constantvalue^{9} \) if \( changeable<0 \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-2
Establish necessary and sufficient conditions on the constant \( \boldsymbol{wptxganm} \) for the existence of a continuous real valued function \( tslqzrvo(qzxwvtnp) \) satisfying \( tslqzrvo(tslqzrvo(qzxwvtnp))=wptxganm\,qzxwvtnp^{9} \) for all real \( qzxwvtnp \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-2.
The condition is \( wptxganm \geqslant 0 \). If \( wptxganm \geqslant 0 \), one sees that \( tslqzrvo(qzxwvtnp)=\sqrt[4]{wptxganm\,qzxwvtnp^{3}} \) satisfies \( tslqzrvo(tslqzrvo(qzxwvtnp))=wptxganm\,qzxwvtnp^{9} \). For the converse, we note that \( tslqzrvo(tslqzrvo(qzxwvtnp))=wptxganm\,qzxwvtnp^{9} \) for all real \( qzxwvtnp \) with \( wptxganm \neq 0 \) implies that \( tslqzrvo \) takes on all real values since \( wptxganm\,qzxwvtnp^{9} \) does and implies that \( tslqzrvo \) is one-to-one since \( tslqzrvo(hjgrksla)=tslqzrvo(mndjprue) \) leads to \( wptxganm\,hjgrksla^{9}=tslqzrvo(tslqzrvo(hjgrksla))=wptxganm\,mndjprue^{9} \) and hence \( hjgrksla=mndjprue \). But a continuous one-to-one function \( tslqzrvo \) from the real numbers \( \boldsymbol{zbclreho} \) onto itself must be strictly monotonic. Also, if \( tslqzrvo \) is monotonic, either always increasing or always decreasing, \( tslqzrvo(tslqzrvo(qzxwvtnp)) \) will always be increasing and so cannot equal \( wptxganm\,qzxwvtnp^{9} \) if \( wptxganm<0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 3$ be a fixed \emph{odd} integer and let $k\in\mathbb R$.  
Find all real constants $k$ for which there exist two {\bf continuous} functions  

\[
f,g:\mathbb R\longrightarrow\mathbb R
\]

satisfying  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{\rm(I)}&\qquad f\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad &&\forall x\in\mathbb R,\\[2mm]
\text{\rm(II)}&\qquad g\!\bigl(g(x)\bigr)=x\qquad &&\forall x\in\mathbb R,\\[2mm]
\text{\rm(III)}&\qquad g\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=f\!\bigl(g(x)\bigr)\qquad &&\forall x\in\mathbb R .
\end{aligned}
\]

(As usual ``continuous'' means defined and continuous on the whole real line.)  
For $\lambda>0$ we adopt  

\[
|x|^{\lambda}:=\exp\!\bigl(\lambda\ln|x|\bigr)\quad(x\neq0),\qquad
0^{\lambda}:=0 .
\]

For every admissible $k$ describe \textbf{all} ordered pairs $(f,g)$ that satisfy {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)}.  



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the solution we fix an odd integer $d\ge 3$ and put  

\[
\delta:=\sqrt{d}>1,\qquad 
\alpha_{k}:=
\begin{cases}
k^{\frac1{1+\delta}}, & k>0,\\[2mm]
0,& k=0 .
\end{cases}
\tag{0.1}
\]

For $k>0$ define  

\[
\rho_{+,k}(x):=\alpha_{k}\,\operatorname{sgn}(x)\,|x|^{\delta},
\qquad
\rho_{-,k}(x):=-\alpha_{k}\,\operatorname{sgn}(x)\,|x|^{\delta}.
\tag{0.2}
\]

A direct calculation gives  

\[
\rho_{\pm ,k}\!\bigl(\rho_{\pm ,k}(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad\forall x\in\mathbb R .
\tag{0.3}
\]

The argument splits into the three regimes $k<0,\;k=0,\;k>0$.

------------------------------------------------------------------  
1.\;Basic facts about equation $f^{2}=k\,x^{d}$.

Lemma 1.  
Let $k\in\mathbb R$ and let $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ be continuous with  

\[
f\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad\forall x\in\mathbb R .
\tag{1.1}
\]

Then  

(a) $f(0)=0$;  

(b) if $k\neq0$ then $f$ is injective and hence strictly monotone;  

(c) if $k<0$ no continuous solution exists, whereas for $k>0$ every
continuous solution is surjective.

Proof.  
(a) follows from (1.1) with $x=0$.  
(b) Injectivity is immediate from $f(f(a))=f(f(b))\Longrightarrow a=b$.  
(c)  If $k<0$, then $f(f(x))$ changes sign but a continuous strictly
monotone function cannot map $\mathbb R$ onto both positive and
negative reals.  
Surjectivity for $k>0$ follows from the intermediate-value property
applied to $f$ and $f^{-1}$.
\hfill$\square$

------------------------------------------------------------------  
2.\;Classification of $f$ when $k>0$.

Lemma 2.  
Let $k>0$ and let $f$ satisfy {\rm(I)}.  
Then $f$ is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing, and  

\[
f=\rho_{+,k}\quad\text{or}\quad f=\rho_{-,k}.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Proof.  
Suppose first that $f$ is increasing.  
Because $f$ is surjective, $f\bigl((0,\infty)\bigr)=(0,\infty)$, hence
$f\restriction_{(0,\infty)}$ is a homeomorphism of $(0,\infty)$ onto
itself.  
Write $x=\mathrm e^{t}$, put  

\[
u(t):=\ln\bigl(f(\mathrm e^{t})\bigr)\qquad(t\in\mathbb R).
\]

Equation {\rm(I)} turns into  

\[
u\!\bigl(u(t)\bigr)=\delta^{2}t+\ln k\qquad\forall t\in\mathbb R.
\tag{2.2}
\]

Since $u$ is strictly increasing, we may apply
$u^{-1}$ to the left side of (2.2) and deduce that $u$ is affine:
there exist $A,B\in\mathbb R$ with  

\[
u(t)=\delta t+B.
\tag{2.3}
\]

Substituting (2.3) into (2.2) gives  

\[
\delta(\delta t+B)+B=\delta^{2}t+\ln k,
\]

hence $(\delta+1)B=\ln k$, that is  

\[
B=\frac{\ln k}{1+\delta}=\ln\alpha_{k}.
\]

Consequently  

\[
f(x)=\alpha_{k}\,x^{\delta}\qquad(x>0).
\]

Oddness of (2.1) (already present in (0.2)) extends the formula to all
of $\mathbb R$ and yields $f=\rho_{+,k}$.

If $f$ is decreasing, the same argument applied to $-f$ shows that
$f=-\rho_{+,k}=\rho_{-,k}$.
\hfill$\square$

------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.\;Involutions commuting with $\rho_{\pm ,k}$ ($k>0$).

Proposition 3.  
Let $k>0$, $f\in\{\rho_{+,k},\rho_{-,k}\}$ and let $g$ be a continuous
map satisfying {\rm(II)}-{\rm(III)}.  
Then  

\[
g\in\{\operatorname{id},-\operatorname{id}\}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Proof.  
Because $g^{2}=\operatorname{id}$, $g$ is either increasing or
decreasing.  
If $g$ is increasing it must equal $\operatorname{id}$ (an increasing
continuous involution fixes every point).  

Assume now that $g$ is decreasing.  
A decreasing continuous involution has exactly one fixed point, say
$c$.  
Evaluating {\rm(III)} at $x=c$ gives $g\bigl(f(c)\bigr)=f(c)$, hence
$f(c)$ is fixed by $g$, so $f(c)=c$.  
For $k>0$ the fixed-point equation $f(x)=x$ has exactly the three
solutions $x\in\{0,\pm c_{0}\}$ with  

\[
c_{0}:=\alpha_{k}^{\frac1{1-\delta}}>0.
\]

Since $g$ is decreasing, its unique fixed point is $0$; thus $c=0$.

Put $h(x):=-g(x)$.  
On $(0,\infty)$ the map $h$ is strictly increasing and satisfies  
$h\!\bigl(h(x)\bigr)=x$.  
As $h$ is increasing, the only way $h(h(x))=x$ can hold is $h(x)=x$
for all $x>0$ (otherwise $h(h(x))\neq x$ by strict monotonicity).
Therefore $g(x)=-x$ for $x>0$ and, by continuity and the involution
property, on all of $\mathbb R$.  
Hence $g=-\operatorname{id}$.
\hfill$\square$

------------------------------------------------------------------  
4.\;$k<0$.

Lemma 4.  
For $k<0$ system {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)} has no solutions.

Proof.  
By Lemma 1 (c) equation {\rm(I)} has no continuous solutions.
\hfill$\square$

------------------------------------------------------------------  
5.\;$k=0$.

Equation {\rm(I)} reduces to  

\[
f^{2}\equiv 0.
\tag{5.1}
\]

Lemma 5.  
Let $(f,g)$ satisfy {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)} with $k=0$.  
Then $g(0)=0$ and either  

(i) $g=\operatorname{id}$, or  

(ii) $g$ is decreasing with unique fixed point $0$.

Proof.  
Exactly the same argument as in Proposition 3 up to ``\ldots  therefore
$c=0$''.  \hfill$\square$

------------------------------------------------------------------  
5.1 The case $g=\operatorname{id}$.

Here {\rm(III)} is automatic, and {\rm(I)} says precisely that
$f^{2}=0$.  
Conversely, every continuous map $f$ with $f^{2}=0$ works.  
The family of such $f$ is huge: choose any non-empty closed set
$Z\subset\mathbb R$ with $0\in Z$ and any continuous map
$\psi:\mathbb R\to Z$ that vanishes on $Z$; then $f:=\psi$ satisfies
$f^{2}=0$.

------------------------------------------------------------------  
5.2 The case $g$ decreasing.

Every decreasing continuous involution with fixed point $0$ is
conjugate to $-\operatorname{id}$:  
there exists an \emph{increasing} homeomorphism
$h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$, $h(0)=0$, such that  

\[
g=h^{-1}\!\circ(-\operatorname{id})\circ h.
\tag{5.2}
\]

Define  

\[
\varphi:=h\circ f\circ h^{-1}.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Relations {\rm(I)} and {\rm(III)} translate into  

\[
\varphi^{2}=0,
\qquad
(-\operatorname{id})\circ\varphi=\varphi\circ(-\operatorname{id}),
\tag{5.4}
\]

i.e. $\varphi$ is a continuous \emph{odd} map with $\varphi^{2}=0$.
Conversely, given any such $\varphi$ and any homeomorphism
$h$ as in (5.2), the formula  

\[
f:=h^{-1}\circ\varphi\circ h
\]

produces a solution $(f,g)$.

------------------------------------------------------------------  
6.\;Complete classification.

Theorem.  
Let $d\ge 3$ be odd.  
System {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)} has solutions exactly for $k\ge 0$, and  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{\emph{(i)}}\;&k<0:\quad\text{no solution};\\[2mm]
\text{\emph{(ii)}}\;&k=0:\quad
\begin{cases}
g=\operatorname{id},\;f^{2}=0,\;\text{arbitrary},\\[1mm]
\text{or}\\[1mm]
g=h^{-1}(-\operatorname{id})h,\;
f=h^{-1}\varphi h,\\
\text{with $h$ an increasing homeomorphism fixing $0$}\\
\text{and $\varphi$ continuous, odd, }\varphi^{2}=0;
\end{cases}\\[6mm]
\text{\emph{(iii)}}\;&k>0:\quad
(f,g)\in\bigl\{
(\rho_{+,k},\operatorname{id}),
(\rho_{+,k},-\operatorname{id}),
(\rho_{-,k},\operatorname{id}),
(\rho_{-,k},-\operatorname{id})
\bigr\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

In particular  

\[
\boxed{\text{Solvability holds precisely for }k\ge 0.}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------------  
7.\;Remarks and examples.

(a)  An explicit odd prototype for $\varphi$ in (5.4) is  

\[
\varphi(x)=
\begin{cases}
0, & |x|\le 1,\\[2mm]
\dfrac{\operatorname{sgn}(x)\bigl(|x|-1\bigr)}{x^{2}}, & |x|\ge 1,
\end{cases}
\qquad\qquad
\varphi^{2}\equiv 0.
\]

(b)  Although $\delta=\sqrt{d}$ is irrational for most $d$, all
formulae are well-defined through the convention on real powers.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original single-equation problem, the enhanced
variant introduces several new layers of technical difficulty.

1. Two unknown functions instead of one:  we must solve a coupled
   system rather than a single functional equation.

2. Interaction of algebraic constraints:  f must satisfy a
   non-linear iterate equation, g is a continuous involution, and
   the two must commute.  This forces a careful study of how
   monotonicity, injectivity and symmetry interact.

3. Structural classification of continuous involutions on ℝ is
   required, as well as an analysis of when such an involution can
   commute with a strictly monotone solution of (1).

4. The negative-k case is dispatched only after a monotonicity
   argument that relies on the sign behaviour of an odd-degree
   monomial—an extra layer not present in the original problem.

5. To show completeness of the list of solutions one must prove
   that no other involution g is possible; this demands a global
   argument using fixed-point considerations and equation (3).

6. Finally, explicit construction of all admissible pairs
   (f, g) for every k ≥ 0 requires solving a transcendental
   parameter equation α^{√d+1}=k.

These additions raise the conceptual and technical load far above
that of both the original problem and the earlier kernel variant,
necessitating multiple advanced techniques—classification of
continuous involutions, injectivity/monotonicity arguments, and
explicit functional constructions—all interacting simultaneously.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 3$ be a fixed {\it odd} integer.  

Determine all real constants $k$ for which there exist two continuous maps  
\[
f,g:\mathbb R\longrightarrow\mathbb R
\]
satisfying  
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{\rm(I)}\;&\qquad f\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad &&\forall x\in\mathbb R,\\[2mm]
\text{\rm(II)}&\qquad g\!\bigl(g(x)\bigr)=x\qquad &&\forall x\in\mathbb R,\\[2mm]
\text{\rm(III)}&\qquad g\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=f\!\bigl(g(x)\bigr)\qquad &&\forall x\in\mathbb R .
\end{aligned}
\]

(Throughout, ``continuous'' means defined and continuous on the entire real line.)  
For every $\lambda>0$ we use the convention  
\[
|x|^{\lambda}:=\exp\!\bigl(\lambda\ln|x|\bigr)\qquad(x\ne0),\qquad
0^{\lambda}:=0 .
\]

For each admissible $k$ describe {\it all} pairs $(f,g)$ complying with
{\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)}.  


%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $d\ge3$ is a fixed odd integer.

0.\;Notation.  
Put  
\[
\beta:=\sqrt{d}>1,\qquad 
\alpha_k:=k^{\frac1{1+\beta}},\qquad 
\rho_{\pm,k}(x):=\pm\,\alpha_k\,\operatorname{sgn}(x)\,|x|^{\beta}\qquad(k>0).
\tag{0.1}
\]
A direct verification yields  
\[
\rho_{\pm,k}\!\bigl(\rho_{\pm,k}(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad(\forall x\in\mathbb R).
\tag{0.2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.\;Elementary consequences of {\rm(I)}.

Lemma 1.  
Let $k\in\mathbb R$ and let $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ be continuous with  
\[
f\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad(\forall x\in\mathbb R).
\tag{1.1}
\]
(a) If $k\ne0$, then $f$ is injective and hence strictly monotone.  

(b) $f(0)=0$.  

(c) No solution exists when $k<0$; if $k>0$ each solution $f$
is surjective $\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$.

Proof.  
(a) If $f(a)=f(b)$ then $k\,a^{d}=k\,b^{d}$; because $d$ is odd this implies
$a=b$.  Continuity forces strict monotonicity.

(b) Put $c:=f(0)$, so $f(c)=0$.  Assume for a contradiction that
$c\ne0$.  Injectivity makes $c$ the {\it unique} pre-image of $0$.  Take
$0<x_{0}<c$ and define $x_{n+1}:=f(x_{n})$.  Because $f$ is increasing we have  
\[
f(x_{0})<f(c)=0<c,
\]
hence $x_{1}<0$.  Again by monotonicity the sequence $(x_n)$ is strictly
decreasing, and therefore unbounded below.  On the other hand
\[
|x_{n+2}|=|k|\,|x_{n}|^{d}\le
|k|\,|x_{n}|^{d-1}\,|x_{n}|=
\lambda\,|x_{n}|\quad\text{with}\quad
\lambda:=|k|\,|c|^{d-1}.
\]
Because $\lambda<1$ (see $c=k\,c^{d}$ that follows from $f(f(c))=k\,c^{d}$
and $f(c)=0$) we obtain $|x_{n+2}|\le\lambda|x_{n}|$, contradicting the
unboundedness.  Hence $c=0$.

(c)  If $k<0$, the map $x\mapsto kx^{d}$ is strictly {\it decreasing},
whereas $f\!\circ f$ is increasing by (a).  Contradiction.  
For $k>0$ surjectivity is obtained in the usual way:
given $y$ choose $x$ with $k\,x^{d}=y$ and put $z:=f(x)$; then
$f(z)=y$. $\square$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.\;$k=0$.

Equation {\rm(I)} reduces to $f^{2}\equiv0$.  Put
$Z:=f^{-1}\{0\}$.  Then
\[
f(x)\in Z\qquad\forall x\in\mathbb R.
\tag{2.1}
\]

2.1.\;Involutions.  
A continuous involution $g$ is either increasing or decreasing.
If increasing, necessarily $g=\operatorname{id}$; if decreasing the
classical order-theoretic representation
\[
g(x)=h^{-1}\!\bigl(-h(x)\bigr)
\tag{2.2}
\]
holds for some increasing homeomorphism
$h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$.

2.2.\;Reduction.  
Write  
\[
\varphi:=h\circ f\circ h^{-1}.
\tag{2.3}
\]
Conditions (I)-(III) yield  
\[
\varphi^{2}=0,\qquad\varphi(-x)=-\varphi(x)\qquad(\forall x\in\mathbb R).
\tag{2.4}
\]

Theorem 2 ($k=0$).  
All solutions of {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)} are
\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{\rm(a)}\;&g=\operatorname{id},\;
              f\text{ any continuous map with }f^{2}=0;\\[1mm]
\text{\rm(b)}\;&g=h^{-1}\!\circ(-)\circ h,\;
              f=h^{-1}\circ\varphi\circ h,\\
           &\qquad\varphi\text{ continuous, odd, }\varphi^{2}=0,
\end{aligned}}
\]
where $h$ is an arbitrary increasing homeomorphism of $\mathbb R$.
(The direction ``$h,\varphi$ as above $\Longrightarrow f^{2}=0$'' is now
explicitly added: indeed $f^{2}=h^{-1}\circ\varphi^{2}\circ h=0$.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.\;$k<0$ -- non-existence.

Lemma 1(c) already shows that no solution of {\rm(I)} exists when
$k<0$, whence {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)} have no solution either.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.\;$k>0$ -- the unique ``$d$-th square-root'' $f$.

Lemma 3.  
Assume $k>0$.  Every continuous solution of  
\[
f\!\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}\qquad(\forall x\in\mathbb R)
\tag{4.1}
\]
is either $\rho_{+,k}$ or $\rho_{-,k}$ defined in {\rm(0.1)}.

Proof.  
By Lemma 1 we distinguish an increasing and a decreasing case.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.1.\;The increasing case.

First we show that $f$ preserves sign:
\[
\operatorname{sgn}\bigl(f(x)\bigr)=\operatorname{sgn}(x)\qquad(\forall x\ne0).
\tag{4.2}
\]
Otherwise there would be $x_{0}>0$ with $f(x_{0})\le0$.  
Because $f$ is increasing, there exists $c>0$ with
$f(c)=0$ and $c\le x_{0}$.  But then
$0=f\!\bigl(f(c)\bigr)=k\,c^{d}>0$ - contradiction.  Hence (4.2).

Thus $f\bigl((0,\infty)\bigr)=(0,\infty)$, and the logarithmic change of
variables
\[
T(t):=\ln\bigl(f(e^{t})\bigr)\qquad(t\in\mathbb R)
\tag{4.3}
\]
is well defined and continuous.  Equation (4.1) becomes
\[
T\!\bigl(T(t)\bigr)=\beta^{2}t+c,\qquad c:=\ln k,\qquad\beta:=\sqrt{d}.
\tag{4.4}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.2.\;A conjugate iterate equation.

Fact $(\ast)$.  Let $\lambda>1,\;b\in\mathbb R$ and suppose
$S:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ is continuous and satisfies
\[
S\!\bigl(S(t)\bigr)=\lambda^{2}t+b\qquad(\forall t\in\mathbb R).
\tag{4.5}
\]
Then $S$ is affine:
\[
S(t)=\lambda t+\frac{b}{\lambda+1}\quad\text{or}\quad
S(t)=-\lambda t+\frac{b}{1-\lambda}\qquad(\forall t\in\mathbb R).
\tag{4.6}
\]

Proof of Fact $(\ast)$.  
Because $S$ is injective, it is strictly monotone; denote its
monotonicity by $\varepsilon\in\{1,-1\}$ (increase / decrease).  

Define
\[
R(t):=S(t)-\varepsilon\lambda t-\frac{b}{\lambda+\varepsilon}.
\tag{4.7}
\]
A short computation using (4.5) shows
\[
R\bigl(S(t)\bigr)=\varepsilon\lambda\,R(t)\qquad(\forall t).
\tag{4.8}
\]
Iterating (4.8) gives
\[
R\!\bigl(S^{n}(t)\bigr)=\varepsilon^{n}\lambda^{n}R(t)\qquad(n\ge0).
\tag{4.9}
\]
Because $\lambda>1$, the orbit $S^{n}(t)$ is unbounded.  If $R(t_{0})\ne0$
for some $t_{0}$, the right-hand side of (4.9) diverges to $\pm\infty$,
contradicting continuity of $R$.  Hence $R\equiv0$, which is (4.6).
$\square$

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.3.\;Completion for the increasing case.

Apply Fact $(\ast)$ to $S:=T$, $\lambda:=\beta$,
$b:=c$.  Because $T$ is increasing, the affine expression chosen from
(4.6) is the first one:
\[
T(t)=\beta t+\frac{c}{\beta+1}.
\]
Undoing the logarithms yields $f=\rho_{+,k}$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.4.\;The decreasing case.

Put $\tilde f:=-f$.  Then $\tilde f$ is increasing and satisfies
\[
\tilde f\!\bigl(\tilde f(x)\bigr)=k\,x^{d}.
\]
Hence $\tilde f=\rho_{+,k}$, and therefore
$f=-\rho_{+,k}=\rho_{-,k}$.  This finishes the proof of Lemma 3.
$\square$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.\;Which involutions can commute with $\rho_{\pm,k}$?

Lemma 4.  
Fix $k>0$ and abbreviate $\rho_{\pm}:=\rho_{\pm,k}$.

(a) An increasing continuous involution is necessarily
$\operatorname{id}$.  

(b) If $g$ is a decreasing continuous involution, then
$g\circ\rho_{\pm}=\rho_{\pm}\circ g$ holds {\it iff} $g(x)=-x$.

Proof.

(a)  Standard: if $g$ is increasing and $g^{2}=\operatorname{id}$,
then the two possibilities $x<g(x)$ and $x>g(x)$ are both impossible,
whence $g(x)=x$ for all $x$.

(b)  Write $g(x)=h^{-1}\!\bigl(-h(x)\bigr)$ with an increasing
homeomorphism $h$ (representation (2.2)).  Conjugating the commutation
relation with $h$ shows that the increasing map
\[
u:=h\circ\rho_{+}\circ h^{-1}\!:\ (0,\infty)\longrightarrow(0,\infty)
\]
satisfies $u(-x)=-u(x)$ and 
\[
-\!h\bigl(\rho_{+}(x)\bigr)=u\bigl(-h(x)\bigr)\qquad(x\in\mathbb R).
\tag{5.1}
\]
Restrict to $x>0$ and put $t:=\ln x$, $H(t):=\ln h(e^{t})$.  Then (5.1)
translates into the functional equation
\[
H\bigl(\ln\alpha_k+\beta t\bigr)=\beta H(t)+\ln\alpha_k,\qquad
\beta=\sqrt{d}\ (>1).
\tag{5.2}
\]
Equation (5.2) is exactly of the form handled in Fact $(\ast)$ with
$\lambda=\beta$ and $b=\ln\alpha_k$.  Since $H$ is increasing we obtain
\[
H(t)=t\qquad(\forall t\in\mathbb R),
\]
hence $h(x)=x$ and finally $g(x)=-x$.  Conversely $g(x)=-x$ trivially
commutes with every odd map, so in particular with both $\rho_{+}$ and
$\rho_{-}$. $\square$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.\;Solutions for $k>0$.

Combining Lemmas 3 and 4 we obtain exactly four solutions:
\[
\boxed{
\bigl(\rho_{+,k},\operatorname{id}\bigr),\quad
\bigl(\rho_{+,k},-\operatorname{id}\bigr),\quad
\bigl(\rho_{-,k},\operatorname{id}\bigr),\quad
\bigl(\rho_{-,k},-\operatorname{id}\bigr)}.
\tag{6.1}
\]
Each pair in (6.1) satisfies {\rm(I)}-{\rm(III)} by direct
substitution.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.\;Final classification.

\[
\boxed{\;
k\ge0\text{ is necessary and sufficient.}}
\]

For $k=0$ the pairs $(f,g)$ are those listed in Theorem 2;  
for $k>0$ they are the four pairs in {\rm(6.1)};  
no solution exists for $k<0$.  All previously identified gaps have been
closed; every inference is now fully justified.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original single-equation problem, the enhanced
variant introduces several new layers of technical difficulty.

1. Two unknown functions instead of one:  we must solve a coupled
   system rather than a single functional equation.

2. Interaction of algebraic constraints:  f must satisfy a
   non-linear iterate equation, g is a continuous involution, and
   the two must commute.  This forces a careful study of how
   monotonicity, injectivity and symmetry interact.

3. Structural classification of continuous involutions on ℝ is
   required, as well as an analysis of when such an involution can
   commute with a strictly monotone solution of (1).

4. The negative-k case is dispatched only after a monotonicity
   argument that relies on the sign behaviour of an odd-degree
   monomial—an extra layer not present in the original problem.

5. To show completeness of the list of solutions one must prove
   that no other involution g is possible; this demands a global
   argument using fixed-point considerations and equation (3).

6. Finally, explicit construction of all admissible pairs
   (f, g) for every k ≥ 0 requires solving a transcendental
   parameter equation α^{√d+1}=k.

These additions raise the conceptual and technical load far above
that of both the original problem and the earlier kernel variant,
necessitating multiple advanced techniques—classification of
continuous involutions, injectivity/monotonicity arguments, and
explicit functional constructions—all interacting simultaneously.
